title: Empires in India
description: Before we go further, let's make sure you've got a handle on some of the key concepts surrounding the Maurya and Gupta empires.

assessment_items.question_content:
**King Ashoka used rock and pillar edicts in order to accomplish which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Ashoka's edicts were written down in language, not pictographs.
The rock and pillar edicts were built within the empire's territory, so they weren't intended to scare off invaders.

choices.content: Write down the stories of the fates of people who had disobeyed the laws
Display laws throughout his empire so people could understand and obey them
Draw pictures so people who couldn’t read could still understand the laws
Write threatening messages to scare off would-be invaders

hints: Pataliputra was not an emperor.  You might be thinking of Chandragupta II.
There were many great scholars and poets in Gupta-era India, but Pataliputra is not one of them.

choices.content: The greatest emperor of the Gupta Empire
The capital city of the Maurya and Gupta Empires
A Mauryan Buddhist philosopher
A Gupta-era scholar and poet

assessment_items.question_content:
In the centuries in between Maurya and Gupta rule, India was led by

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: _Arthashastra_ wasn't a king; it was a text about running countries and waging wars.
Magadha was an ancient kingdom that predated the Maurya Empire; it didn't come between the Mauryas and the Guptas.

choices.content: the Magadha empire
many kings taking charge of small areas
King Arthashastra
a series of Brahmins, Buddhist priests

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, revisit this passage from the article:

> "After gaining power, Chandragupta II expanded the Gupta Empire through conquest and political marriages until the end of his reign in 413 CE. By 395 CE, his control over India extended coast-to-coast. Just like Ashoka, Chandragupta II made Pataliputra the capital of his empire and centralized the government there. He used tribute money from allies to fund government projects and salaries. Unlike Ashoka, Chandragupta did not rely on a network of spies or closely monitor the affairs of foreigners or allies. Instead, he let regions make their own decisions about administration and local governance. 

> Some scholars have argued that the Gupta empire was a “golden age” of India. The empire was marked by peace and public safety, and scholars flourished in this environment. Kalidasa, a poet of the time, is considered the greatest poet and dramatist of the Sanskrit language; Aryabhata was the first of the Indian mathematician-astronomers who worked on the approximation for Pi; and Vishnu Sharma is thought to be the author of the Panchatantra fables, one of the most widely-translated non-religious books in history."

**What might a historian argue about the relationship between imperial stability and the rise of scholarship?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The text indicates that there is a definite relationship between imperial stability and scholarship.
The historical record does not contain many instances of wealthy people being forced into service as scholars by an emperor.

choices.content: An empire at peace, with many tributaries and colonies, has resources to support a wealthy, educated population
In times of peace, emperors often force their wealthier citizens into positions as scholars
Good scholarship results in an increase in political and imperial stability
There is no relationship between imperial stability and scholarship

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, revisit this passage from the article:

> "After gaining power, Chandragupta II expanded the Gupta Empire through conquest and political marriages until the end of his reign in 413 CE. By 395 CE, his control over India extended coast-to-coast. Just like Ashoka, Chandragupta II made Pataliputra the capital of his empire and centralized the government there. He used tribute money from allies to fund government projects and salaries. Unlike Ashoka, Chandragupta did not rely on a network of spies or closely monitor the affairs of foreigners or allies. Instead, he let regions make their own decisions about administration and local governance. 

> Some scholars have argued that the Gupta empire was a “golden age” of India. The empire was marked by peace and public safety, and scholars flourished in this environment. Kalidasa, a poet of the time, is considered the greatest poet and dramatist of the Sanskrit language; Aryabhata was the first of the Indian mathematician-astronomers who worked on the approximation for Pi; and Vishnu Sharma is thought to be the author of the Panchatantra fables, one of the most widely-translated non-religious books in history."


**What might be one __disadvantage__ of an emperor letting regions make their own decisions about how they govern themselves?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Self-governing provinces are not likely to cease agricultural production as a result of their independence.
Lessening the administrative strain on the central empire doesn't seem like a *disadvantage*.

choices.content: Regions could grow and strengthen as their own entities, eventually posing a threat to the empire
Regions would most likely stop producing agriculture or trading with other regions
Self-governing regions would be less of an administrative strain on the empire
Self-governing regions might be more subject to attack from outside forces
